subject: Chemistry
options: At $$600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}$$ can reduce $$\mathrm{FeO}$$
At $$600{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}$$ can reduce $$\mathrm{ZnO}$$
At $$600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{CO}$$ can reduce $$\mathrm{ZnO}$$
At $$600{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{CO}$$ cannot reduce $$\mathrm{FeO}$$
solution:
The Ellingham diagram is a graphical representation of the Gibbs Free Energy change of various reactions, mainly reduction reactions of metal oxides to the metal. The temperature is plotted on the x-axis, and the Gibbs Free Energy per mole of O2 is plotted on the y-axis.

In the Ellingham diagram, each reaction's line has a positive slope because the entropy change for the reactions is positive (we're converting a solid and a gas into more moles of gas), and the Gibbs free energy change, ΔG, is given by ΔG = ΔH - TΔS. Therefore, the ΔG becomes more negative as T increases.

It's important to note that a more negative ΔG indicates a more spontaneous reaction, i.e., the reaction is more likely to occur. Thus, substances lower on the Ellingham diagram are more likely to reduce the substances above them.

In the diagram provided, the line for C is below the line for FeO at 600°C. This implies that Carbon can reduce Iron(II) oxide to Iron at this temperature.

Therefore, the statement "At $$600^{\circ} \mathrm{C}, \mathrm{C}$$ can reduce $$\mathrm{FeO}$$" is correct.
title: JEE Main 2023 (Online) 10th April Evening Shift

subject: Chemistry
options: Both $$\mathbf{A}$$ and $$\mathbf{R}$$ are true and $$\mathbf{R}$$ is the correct explanation of $$\mathbf{A}$$
$$\mathbf{A}$$ is false but $$\mathbf{R}$$ is true
Both $$\mathbf{A}$$ and $$\mathbf{R}$$ are true but $$\mathbf{R}$$ is NOT the correct explanation of $$\mathbf{A}$$
$$\mathbf{A}$$ is true but $$\mathbf{R}$$ is false
solution:
In the Ellingham diagram, a sharp change in slope of the line is observed for $\mathrm{Mg}-\mathrm{MgO}$ at $\sim 1120^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ because that is the boiling point of magnesium.

There is a large increase in entropy associated with the change of state of magnesium. So, both Assertion (A) and Reason (R) are true and (R) is the correct explanation of $(A)$.

subject: Chemistry
options: A is correct but R is not correct
Both A and R are correct but R is NOT the correct explanation of A
A is not correct but R is correct
Both A and R are correct and R is the correct explanation of A
solution:
$$ 2 \mathrm{C}(\mathrm{s})+\mathrm{O}_2(\mathrm{~g}) \rightarrow 2 \mathrm{CO}(\mathrm{g}) $$

$\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}^0$ is $+v e, \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{G}^0=\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{H}^0-\mathrm{T} \Delta_{\mathrm{r}} \mathrm{S}^0$;

Thus slope is negative.

As temperature increases $\Delta_{\mathrm{r}} G^{\mathrm{o}}$ becomes more negative thus it has lower tendency to get decomposed.
title: JEE Main 2023 (Online) 1st February Morning Shift

subject: Chemistry
options: Both Statement I and Statement II are correct.
Both Statement I and Statement II are incorrect.
Statement I is correct but Statement II is incorrect.
Statement I is incorrect but Statement II is correct.
solution:
Ellingham diagram is plot of $$\Delta $$G vs T. The criterion for the feasibility of a thermal reduction is that at a given temperature Gibbs energy change of a reaction must be negative. The change in Gibbs energy, $$\Delta $$G for any process at any specified temperature is given by the equation

$$\Delta $$G = $$\Delta $$H - T$$\Delta $$S

where $$\Delta $$H = enthalpy change and $$\Delta $$S = entropy change

According to the ellingham diagram, any metal oxide with higher $$\Delta $$G° has a tendency of getting reduced by the metal whose metal oxide has lower value of $$\Delta $$G°.

intersection represents ΔG = 0 and the temperature
at which sudden increase in the slope occurs is
indicated by melting or boiling.

solution:

Given Statement II is false as the value of $$\Delta$$S is decreases from left to right in Ellingham diagram.
